quiz.title: Macroeconomics (Quiz 1)
quiz.subtitle: Unit 3 Quiz 1: Aggregate Demand & Aggregate Supply (20 Questions)
quiz.subject: Economics
quiz.topic: AD-AS Model, Long-Run Aggregate Supply, Short-Run Aggregate Supply

questions.question: The Aggregate Demand (AD) curve shows the relationship between the price level and:
options: The quantity of real GDP demanded
The nominal interest rate
The unemployment rate
The money supply
questions.explanation: The AD curve plots the total quantity of goods and services demanded in an economy (Real GDP) at various price levels.

questions.question: Why does the Aggregate Demand curve slope downward? SELECT ALL THAT APPLY
options: The Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility
The Wealth Effect
Relationship between interest rate and price
appreciation and depreciation of currency
The Law of Supply
Sticky wages in the short run
questions.explanation: A lower price level increases real wealth (Wealth Effect), lowers interest rates (Interest Rate Effect), and depreciates the currency (Exchange Rate Effect), all of which increase the quantity of real GDP demanded.

questions.question: Which of the following would cause the Aggregate Demand curve to shift to the right?
options: A decrease in consumer confidence
An increase in personal income taxes
The central bank increasing the money supply
A decrease in government spending on infrastructure
questions.explanation: An increase in the money supply lowers interest rates, which stimulates investment and consumption spending, increasing AD at every price level (rightward shift).

questions.question: The aggregate supply curve in the long run (LRAS) is ______________
options: Downward sloping because a lower price level increases real wealth, consumption, and output
Upward sloping because higher prices increase profits and output, and because increase in nominal wage puts a pressure on price
Vertical because output is determined by resources, technology, and institutions, not the price level
Horizontal because prices are fixed and firms supply whatever is demanded at that price level
questions.explanation: LRAS represents potential GDP (Y*). It is independent of the price level because all prices and wages are fully flexible in the long run. Output is determined by real factors: resources, technology, and institutions.

questions.question: Which of the following would shift the Long-Run Aggregate Supply curve to the left?
options: An increase in the labor force participation rate
A major natural disaster destroying capital stock
An advancement in technology
An appreciation of the domestic currency
questions.explanation: LRAS shifts based on changes in the factors of production. Destruction of capital reduces the economy's productive capacity, shifting LRAS leftward.

questions.question: The Short-Run Aggregate Supply (SRAS) curve is upward sloping primarily due to:
options: Menu costs and money illusion
The law of increasing opportunity cost
Sticky wages and sticky prices
Perfectly flexible input prices
questions.explanation: In the short run, nominal wages and some input prices are fixed ('sticky'). As the price level rises, firms' revenues increase faster than costs, so they produce more output.

questions.question: Stagflation is best represented in the AD-AS model by:
options: A rightward shift of the AD curve
A leftward shift of the SRAS curve
A rightward shift of the LRAS curve
A movement up along the AD curve
questions.explanation: A negative supply shock (e.g., sharp rise in oil prices) shifts SRAS left. This results in a higher price level (inflation) and lower real GDP (recession).

questions.question: What happens to the price level and real GDP in the short run if consumer optimism increases significantly?
options: Price level falls; Real GDP falls
Price level rises; Real GDP rises
Price level rises; Real GDP falls
Price level remains unchanged; Real GDP rises
questions.explanation: Increased optimism shifts AD to the right. In the short run, the economy moves up along the SRAS curve, resulting in higher prices (inflation) and higher output.

questions.question: In the long run, if the economy is currently operating below potential GDP (a recessionary gap), what will happen if there is no government intervention?
options: AD will shift left to restore equilibrium
SRAS will shift right due to falling nominal wages
LRAS will shift left to match current output
The economy will remain in recession permanently
questions.explanation: With high unemployment, nominal wages will eventually fall. This reduces production costs, shifting SRAS rightward until the economy returns to potential GDP at a lower price level.

question.question: Stagflation is often the result of ________________
options: cost-push inflation
demand-pull inflation
increase in aggregate supply
decrease in aggregate demand
question.explanation: Stagflation—a combination of stagnant economic growth (falling real GDP) and high inflation (rising price level)—is caused by cost-push inflation. This occurs when the SRAS curve shifts left due to rising input costs such as oil or wages, increasing prices while reducing output.

question.question: Which of the following is a determinant of Aggregate Supply, but NOT a determinant of Aggregate Demand?
options: Exchange rates
Household wealth
Nominal wage rates
Business taxes
question.explanation: Nominal wages affect the cost of production, which shifts the SRAS curve. While business taxes can affect investment (AD), wage rates specifically target the supply side.

question.question: An increase in the price level causes a movement ______ along the AD curve, while a decrease in net exports causes the AD curve to ______.
options: Up and to the left; Shift left
Down and to the right; Shift left
Up and to the left; Shift right
Down and to the right; Shift right
question.explanation: Price level changes cause movements (Quantity of RGDP demanded changes). A drop in net exports is a non-price level determinant that decreases overall spending at every price level (Leftward shift).

question.question: In the AD-AS model, the LRAS curve implies that expansionary fiscal policy in the long run will result in:
options: Higher output and lower prices
Higher prices and no change in output
Lower prices and higher output
No change in prices or output
question.explanation: Starting at full employment, a rightward shift in AD (due to fiscal policy) increases the price level. Since LRAS is vertical, output cannot permanently exceed potential GDP.

question.question: Which event would cause a movement up along a stationary SRAS curve?
options: An increase in the price of oil
A decrease in the expected price level
An increase in government spending
A technological breakthrough
question.explanation: An increase in government spending shifts AD right. The economy moves up and to the right *along* the existing SRAS curve (higher price level, higher output). Options A and D *shift* the SRAS curve.

question.question: If the US dollar appreciates against foreign currencies, what is the impact on US Aggregate Demand?
options: AD shifts right because imports are cheaper
AD shifts left because net exports decrease
AD shifts right because exports increase
Movement down along the AD curve
question.explanation: A stronger dollar makes US goods more expensive for foreigners (exports fall) and foreign goods cheaper for Americans (imports rise). Net Exports (X-M) fall, shifting AD left.

question.question: In the short-run, an overheated economy — with producers 'smiling to the bank' — may subsequently experience which of the following? SELECT ALL THAT APPLY
options: Cost-push pressure due to costlier raw materials
Cost-push pressure due to requests for higher nominal wages
Contractionary fiscal policy measures by the government
Contractionary monetary policy measures by the central bank
A wage-price spiral if the overheating persists into the long-run
question.explanation: An overheated economy (inflationary gap) creates upward pressure on costs. 1) Input prices and wages rise as demand exceeds potential (A & B). 2) Policy makers may intervene with contractionary fiscal or monetary policy to cool the economy (C & D). 3) If not corrected, the cycle of rising wages and prices leads to a wage-price spiral (E).

question.question: Which of the following shifts the Short-Run Aggregate Supply curve to the right?
options: An increase in the expected future price level
A decrease in the availability of raw materials
A temporary decrease in nominal wages
An increase in sales taxes on businesses
question.explanation: Lower nominal wages reduce production costs. Firms are willing to supply more output at any given price level, shifting SRAS to the right.

question.question: The economy is in long-run equilibrium. A stock market crash destroys trillions in household wealth. In the short run, we expect:
options: Price level up, Real GDP up
Price level down, Real GDP down
Price level up, Real GDP down
Price level down, Real GDP up
question.explanation: Wealth destruction reduces consumption, shifting AD left. This results in a recessionary gap with lower prices and lower output in the short run.

question.question: SELECT ALL THAT APPLY: Which of the following is associated with demand-pull inflation?
options: A rightward shift of the demand-curve during full employment
A rightward shift of the demand-curve when near full employment
Potential GDP being less than Actual GDP
Potential GDP being equal to Actual GDP
Removal of subsidies for producers
question.explanation: Demand-pull inflation occurs when aggregate demand increases faster than aggregate supply, pulling prices up. This happens when AD shifts right at or near full employment (A, B), or when actual GDP exceeds potential GDP, creating an inflationary gap (C). Option D is long-run equilibrium with no inflationary pressure. Option E is a supply-side cost increase, causing cost-push inflation.

question.question: If the government increases spending on highways and bridges, which curve shifts first, and in what direction?
options: SRAS shifts right
AD shifts right
LRAS shifts left
AD shifts left
question.explanation: Government spending is a direct component of Aggregate Demand (G in C+I+G+NX). The immediate impact is a rightward shift of the AD curve. (Over a long period, better infrastructure might shift LRAS, but AD shifts first).

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have a strong command of the AD-AS model and can distinguish between movements along curves and shifts of curves.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! You understand the core dynamics of the model. Review the specific effects of exchange rates and the difference between SRAS and LRAS shifts.
scoreMessages.50: A fair attempt. Focus on: 1) AD slopes down (Wealth/Interest Rate effects). 2) AD shifts (C, I, G, NX). 3) SRAS shifts (Input costs, productivity). 4) LRAS is vertical at potential output.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Remember: Rightward AD shift = Higher Prices, Higher Output. Rightward SRAS shift = Lower Prices, Higher Output. LRAS only moves when the economy's capacity changes.
